does: This paper describes OpenAI's framework for engaging independent external evaluators to test frontier AI systems before deployment. The approach involves collaborating with third-party experts to conduct adversarial testing, red-teaming, and capability assessments on large language models. The work documents concrete practices for external testing partnerships, including the scope of evaluations conducted, methods for identifying and verifying safety risks, and mechanisms for incorporating external findings into deployment decisions. The paper reports how this external testing has validated existing internal safety measures, uncovered previously unknown vulnerabilities, and provided evidence about model capabilities and limitations that informed release decisions.
connects: This work builds on established safety evaluation practices in AI development while responding to growing calls for independent oversight of frontier models. It extends earlier internal red-teaming methodologies by formalizing partnerships with external researchers who bring diverse perspectives and expertise. The approach connects to broader efforts in AI governance and transparency, including responses to concerns that proprietary model developers cannot adequately self-assess their own systems. The paper engages with literature on algorithmic auditing, third-party verification in technology, and the role of external accountability mechanisms in ensuring responsible AI deployment. It also reflects lessons from testing practices in other high-stakes domains like pharmaceuticals and aviation where independent evaluation is standard.
enables: This framework establishes a template for systematic external testing that other AI developers and regulators could adopt. The work points toward more institutionalized third-party evaluation as a standard practice in frontier AI deployment, potentially supporting the development of testing standards and accreditation for AI evaluators. It enables future research on scaling external evaluation processes, designing adversarial tests that generalize across different model architectures, and creating feedback loops between external findings and model improvement. The approach also supports the emergence of independent AI safety evaluation as a specialized field, creating opportunities for security researchers and domain experts to contribute to AI safety assessment without direct access to model weights or training data.
